slides.heading: The Science of Sleep Cycles
slides.body: Why 8 hours isn't enough — and what your brain actually needs at night

slides.heading: Sleep Is Not One Thing
slides.body: Sleep isn't a single uniform state. Your brain cycles through distinct stages throughout the night, each serving a different biological purpose. Missing any stage has real consequences.

slides.heading: The 90-Minute Cycle
slides.body: Each sleep cycle lasts roughly 90 minutes and contains four stages: N1 (light sleep), N2 (onset sleep), N3 (deep sleep), and REM. A full night typically means 4–6 complete cycles.

slides.heading: N3: Deep Sleep Repairs the Body
slides.body: Slow-wave deep sleep (N3) is when your body releases growth hormone, repairs tissue, and consolidates physical memories. It dominates the first half of the night — cutting sleep short robs you of it.

slides.heading: REM: The Brain's Night Shift
slides.body: REM (Rapid Eye Movement) sleep is when your brain processes emotions, consolidates learning, and runs creative problem-solving. REM cycles get longer toward morning — sleeping in matters.

slides.heading: Why Timing Beats Duration
slides.body: Waking mid-cycle — especially out of N3 or REM — causes sleep inertia, that groggy, disoriented feeling. Apps and smart alarms that track movement try to wake you at the end of a cycle instead.

slides.heading: The Glymphatic System Cleans at Night
slides.body: During deep sleep, the brain's glymphatic system activates, flushing out metabolic waste including amyloid-beta — a protein linked to Alzheimer's. Chronic poor sleep literally lets toxins accumulate.

slides.heading: Alcohol & Screens Hijack Your Cycles
slides.body: Alcohol suppresses REM sleep, leaving you emotionally raw the next day. Blue light from screens delays melatonin release by up to 3 hours, pushing back the start of your entire sleep architecture.

slides.heading: Napping: Use It Wisely
slides.body: A 20-minute nap boosts alertness without entering deep sleep (avoiding grogginess). A 90-minute nap completes one full cycle and meaningfully restores cognitive performance. Avoid napping after 3pm.

slides.heading: Sleep Smarter, Not Just Longer
slides.body: Quality sleep means completing full 90-minute cycles, protecting deep sleep and REM, and waking at the right moment. Consistent sleep and wake times are the single most powerful habit for better rest.
